artifacts.content:
Hi Angela,

I wanted to check in before our Thursday meeting and try to put some of this into words while it’s fresh. The last few weeks I’ve been thinking a lot about how far things are from where they were back in February 2022, when I lost that Midtown retail job and ended up in the Queens Community Mental Health Center’s inpatient unit for those 5 days. I still remember sitting in that little room on the unit with Dr. Patel going over the discharge summary and talking about the suicidal thoughts and bumping up my SSRI dose. I don’t ever want to get that close to that place again.

Most days now feel much more stable, but I can feel the winter heaviness creeping in. I’m proud that since March 2023 I’ve been consistent with the meds the nurse practitioner put me on — the sertraline 150 mg every morning, and the clonazepam 0.5 mg only when I really need it for panic attacks. Sometimes on the M train, if it’s crowded and stops between stations, I feel that familiar rush and I’ll take one. I still get nervous that someone at Bright Pages will notice the clonazepam bottle in my bag, especially Cara, after that “Attendance Concerns – October Shifts” email in October 2023 when I called out three times because of those panic attacks on the way to Williamsburg.

I keep thinking about that argument with Marco last summer in the Woodside kitchen, when he said, “We can’t keep having you on the couch forever,” and mentioned shelters if I couldn’t move out by the end of the year. It’s part of why I got so desperate this past January and stayed late at the bookstore after closing, hiding in the back office and Googling “NYC women’s shelter intake Ridgewood” on the work computer. I felt like such a burden on Elena’s family.

Can we use our session this week to make a really concrete plan for the next few months? Like specific steps for housing, and also what I should do if I notice myself slipping toward that December 2022 place again when I went almost a week without showering on the couch and Elena said in Spanish that we might need another hospital stay. I’d like to have it written down so when my brain gets foggy I can just follow the steps.

Thank you for sticking with me through all of this.

Isabel

metadata.subject: Planning for the next few months

artifacts.content:
Personal note – do not share

What I can handle on my own right now:
- Basic stuff: I wake up around 7:30 a.m. without anyone dragging me out of bed (most days). Shower, get dressed, oatmeal or eggs, make coffee, pack my bag.
- Travel: Q58 to the M to the L, or just the M/L if I’m already in Queens. I can use Google Maps if I get turned around.
- Work: I’ve kept the Bright Pages Books job, even after that written warning from Cara in October 2023 (“Attendance Concerns – October Shifts” in my Gmail). The panic attacks on the M train were awful, but I pushed through, and now I’m better at leaving earlier so I don’t feel rushed.
- Money: SSI has been steady since late 2021. Back when things were rougher, Mami basically acted like a payee through that credit union account in Jackson Heights and kind of controlled how much cash I could take out because she was scared I’d overspend when I was depressed. It felt infantilizing then, but I also get why she did it. Now I check my own balance and keep a simple budget in this notebook.

Where I still struggle:
- Paper and deadlines: I think about Spring 2015 at LaGuardia when I ignored all those “Urgent: Risk of Academic Dismissal – Liberal Arts Program” emails and ended up failing two classes and unofficially withdrawing from another. That’s still my pattern when I get overwhelmed: avoid, avoid, avoid.
- Mental health dips: December 2022 is burned into my brain — almost a week on the pull-out couch without showering, barely moving, until Elena sat on the edge and told me in Spanish that if I didn’t start taking care of myself we might have to think about another hospital stay like the 5-day one in Feb 2022 at the Queens Community Mental Health Center unit.

Reminder to future me: you are not back there. You take your sertraline 150 mg every morning. The clonazepam 0.5 mg is there if the panic spikes, not something to be ashamed of. You survived all of that and you’re still here, still trying.

Hi Cara,

I hope you’re having a good week and that the new front table display isn’t driving you too crazy yet. I wanted to touch base about my schedule and also be honest about a few things, since you’ve been really fair with me.

I still think about that email you sent in October 2023, “Attendance Concerns – October Shifts,” after I called out three times in one month. At the time I was having bad panic attacks on the M train on the way from Queens, sometimes stuck between stations with my heart racing so fast I thought I was going to pass out. Since then my nurse practitioner at Queens Community Mental Health Center adjusted my meds — I’m on sertraline 150 mg every morning and have clonazepam 0.5 mg to take only if I feel a panic attack coming on. It’s helped a lot, and I’ve also been leaving earlier so I don’t feel rushed.

I’m telling you this because I really value the job and I don’t want you to think I don’t take it seriously. I’m careful about the clonazepam (and I keep the bottle buried in my bag because I’m weirdly afraid a coworker will see it and think I’m not reliable). I also had a rough patch a couple years ago after I lost a Midtown retail job and ended up with a 5-day inpatient stay at the Queens Community Mental Health Center’s affiliated unit in February 2022. That was kind of a wake-up call for me about how much stress and chaos can throw me off.

Right now I’m staying with my sister’s family in Woodside, still on the pull-out couch, and things feel crowded and tense sometimes. Last summer my brother-in-law Marco and I had an argument in the kitchen where he basically said, “We can’t keep having you on the couch forever,” and mentioned shelters if I couldn’t move out. That scared me enough that this January I actually stayed late after closing a few times and sat in the back office Googling “NYC women’s shelter intake Ridgewood” on the store computer, just trying to understand my options.

I’m working with my therapist and case manager on finding a more stable place to live so I can keep showing up for work consistently. In the meantime, if you ever notice a pattern in my attendance again, please tell me early so I can course-correct before it gets to the warning stage.

Thanks for reading this long message and for giving me a chance to grow here.

Best,
Isabel

metadata.subject: Checking in about schedule and stability

artifacts.content:
Brain dump – housing + independence

I keep coming back to this idea: “mostly independent with some support.” It’s true. I can get up, shower, cook arroz con gandules or simple chicken and veggies, do laundry in the basement on Sundays, get myself to work in Williamsburg. I can travel alone on the M and L trains and the Q58 bus. I can keep my own little budget: SSI + Bright Pages pay, cash to Elena for groceries and utilities.

But there are these other pieces:

Money history: Since late 2021, when the SSI first started, Mami basically turned into my unofficial representative payee. She helped me set up that account at the credit union in Jackson Heights and for a few months in 2022 she decided how much cash I could withdraw. She was scared I’d overspend during a depressive spell. It hurt my pride, but looking back, I was pretty fragile then.

School: Spring 2015 at LaGuardia is my cautionary tale. Depression hit, I stopped going to classes, and I ignored all those emails from my advisor with subject lines like “Urgent: Risk of Academic Dismissal – Liberal Arts Program.” Result: failed two classes, unofficially withdrew from another, and felt like a complete failure.

Mental health: February 2022 — losing the Midtown job, then the 5-day inpatient stay at Queens Community Mental Health Center’s unit with Dr. Patel writing in the discharge summary about suicidal thoughts and raising my SSRI dose. December 2022 — almost a week without showering on the pull-out couch in Woodside until Elena sat down and told me in Spanish that if I didn’t start taking care of myself again, we might have to think about another hospital stay.

Now: sertraline 150 mg every morning since around March 2023, clonazepam 0.5 mg as needed for panic. I’m mostly steady. I just need structure: reminders, check-ins, a quiet space with a door I can close so I don’t slide back.

Goal: own small apartment (Ridgewood?), still near QCMHC, Bright Pages, and family. Prove to myself I can pay rent on time and keep going to therapy.

Event: Weekly therapy with Dr. Patel

Time: Tuesday, March 12, 2024 – 3:00 p.m. to 3:50 p.m.
Location: Queens Community Mental Health Center, 3rd floor, Dr. Patel’s office

Notes to self:
- Bring mood/sleep notebook and the little budget notebook.
- Talk about winter slump and how it’s different from December 2022 (that week on the pull-out couch without showering until Elena sat down and told me in Spanish we might need to think about another hospital stay).
- Revisit February 2022 inpatient stay at the Queens Community Mental Health Center’s affiliated unit – what were the warning signs before those 5 days, and how can I catch them earlier now? Remember: lost Midtown retail job, suicidal thoughts, SSRI dose increased.
- Update her on meds: sertraline 150 mg every morning since March 2023, clonazepam 0.5 mg as needed for panic (esp. on the M train). Mention that I still worry about coworkers at Bright Pages seeing the clonazepam bottle in my bag.
- Bring up that October 2023 “Attendance Concerns – October Shifts” email from Cara about calling out sick three times because of panic attacks on the M train. How to keep work from getting shaky again.
- Housing stress: still on the couch at Elena and Marco’s in Woodside. Remember to mention the kitchen argument last summer when Marco said, “We can’t keep having you on the couch forever,” and hinted about shelters.
- Also tell her about those nights in January 2024 staying late in the Bright Pages back office, writing in my notebook about feeling like a burden and Googling “NYC women’s shelter intake Ridgewood” on the store computer.

Goal for session: leave with a clear safety/relapse plan and 2–3 concrete housing steps for the next month.

Elena 💬

Elena: Oye, are you getting home for dinner tonight or working late again?

Isabel: I’ll be home. Cara only put me on until 6, so I should be back in Woodside by 7:30 if the M train behaves.

Elena: Good. I’m making arroz con gandules and baked chicken. Sofia wants to show you her science project.

Isabel: Aww, tell her I can’t wait.

Elena: You’ve been so quiet lately. Everything okay? You’re not slipping like in December 2022, right? 😕

Isabel: I’m not back there, I promise. I remember that week on the pull-out couch too well, not showering and you sitting down and saying we might need another hospital stay if I didn’t start taking care of myself. I don’t want that again.

Elena: Me neither. You scared me.

Isabel: I’m keeping up with meds. Sertraline 150 mg every morning, and the clonazepam 0.5 mg only if my anxiety spikes, like on the M train. The nurse practitioner at Queens Community Mental Health Center is watching it.

Elena: Ok. Just don’t take too many of those. You know how I worry.

Isabel: I know. Also talked to Angela about how things still feel weird since last summer when Marco and I argued in the kitchen and he said, “We can’t keep having you on the couch forever,” and mentioned shelters. That’s still stuck in my head.

Elena: He shouldn’t have said it like that. He was stressed. But yeah, we do need more space eventually.

Isabel: I get it. That’s partly why in January I was staying late at Bright Pages after closing, hiding in the back office and Googling “NYC women’s shelter intake Ridgewood” on the store computer. I felt like such a burden.

Elena: Ay, Isa. Why didn’t you tell me that?

Isabel: I didn’t want to make you worry more. I’m working on housing stuff with Angela and Dr. Patel now. I just need you to keep being honest with me, not just mad like Marco was.

Elena: I’m here. Just come home for dinner. We’ll figure it out together.

Hi Gaby,

I’ve been meaning to write you a real update instead of just reacting to your memes, haha. I’m at the café near the bookstore on my break, so here goes.

Work is okay. I’m still at Bright Pages in Williamsburg. You remember last fall when I freaked out because Cara sent that “Attendance Concerns – October Shifts” email? That was after three mornings of full-on panic attacks on the M train and me calling out sick. Since then the nurse practitioner at Queens Community Mental Health Center adjusted my meds (sertraline 150 mg every morning, plus clonazepam 0.5 mg only when the panic gets bad), and it’s been more manageable.

Home is…complicated. Still on the pull-out couch at Elena and Marco’s in Woodside. I love the kids, but there’s zero privacy. Last summer Marco and I had that big fight in the kitchen — he said, “We can’t keep having you on the couch forever,” and brought up shelters if I couldn’t move out. It hurt so much I didn’t even tell you then. It stuck with me, though. In January this year, I stayed late after closing a few nights, sitting alone in the Bright Pages back office, writing in my notebook about feeling like “a burden on Elena’s family” and Googling “NYC women’s shelter intake Ridgewood” on the store computer. Just in case.

I keep thinking about how different things are from a few years ago. Like Spring 2015 at LaGuardia when my depression got bad and I stopped going to classes, ignored all those “Urgent: Risk of Academic Dismissal – Liberal Arts Program” emails, and ended up failing two classes and unofficially withdrawing from another. And then February 2022, when I lost that Midtown retail job and landed in the Queens Community Mental Health Center inpatient unit for 5 days. Dr. Patel wrote “suicidal ideation” in the discharge summary and bumped up my meds. I still hate thinking about that.

The good news: I haven’t missed therapy or meds in months. Angela (my case manager) is helping me look into places in Ridgewood so I can have my own little apartment but still be near everyone and the clinic. I just want a bed that isn’t a couch and a door that closes.

Anyway, enough heavy stuff. How’s Bushwick life treating you? Still dragging your plants out on the fire escape for “sunbathing”? Send pics.

Love you,
Isabel

Hi Elena,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to update you on the housing opportunity we talked about. My case manager, Angela Martinez, mentioned Ridgewood Supportive Housing to me recently. She got an email from the housing coordinator at the clinic about some openings in supportive apartments in the area. Angela thought it would be a good fit for me, considering the level of support they offer and the location. It’s a relief to think about having my own space, especially on days when the living room feels a bit too crowded. 

After Angela told me, I checked it out online and found a description on the agency’s website. I also saw a flyer pinned up on the bulletin board in the clinic’s lobby. During our weekly appointment, Angela encouraged me to apply, as she believes it could be the right step toward more independence. 

In terms of contact, if they need to reach me, they can use my cell at (347) 555-8142 or our home phone at (718) 555-2398. I’m using your email here since it might be easier for follow-ups, but they can also contact me directly at isabel.rodriguez.queens@gmail.com if needed.

I’m really hoping this works out. Having my own one-bedroom apartment in Ridgewood would mean still being close to everything—work at Bright Pages Books, the clinic, and of course, you and the kids. Plus, knowing there are staff available for check-ins would give me peace of mind if I start feeling overwhelmed.

Thank you for all your support, Elena. I really appreciate having you and Marco around, especially with Sofia and Daniel keeping things lively. Please let me know your thoughts, and if you have any questions or ideas, I’m always here to chat.

Love, 
Isabel
